answer:
**Manusmṛti chapter 5 primarily addresses rules for purity, forbidden and permissible foods (especially meats), and conditions for eating meat in sacrifices or emergencies (e.g., Mn_5.4–5.41).**[^1]

It does not mention funeral offerings (such as *śrāddha* or piṇḍa rites), cooking them, or any prescription for specific wood (e.g., śamī, palāśa/samī/palāśa, or firewood/fuel) to kindle the fire for such cooking.[^2] Ancestral rites (*śrāddha*) appear elsewhere (e.g., chapter 3 or 4), but no such detail on wood is found there or in targeted searches across the work.[^3]

The corpus does not cover this specific prescription—if it exists in traditional interpretations, it is absent here.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 5, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/05/translation/).

[^2]: Searches for "wood," "firewood," "fuel," "śamī," "palāśa," "cook," "fire," + "śrāddha"/"pinda"/"funeral" + "Manusmṛti"/"chapter 5" yielded no matches.

[^3]: E.g., *Manusmṛti* 4.223 mentions *śrāddha*-performing Shudras but no cooking/fire details, [paragraph](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-00b464). Chapter 3 discusses householder duties including some ancestral rites but no wood specification, [full chapter](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/).
